title: The Drip

jokes.text: Is it blood? No! There's... there is leak in the ceiling.
jokes.setup: Johnny wakes up soaking wet and asks if it's blood
jokes.punchline: It's just a mundane ceiling leak, not something dramatic
jokes.explanation: Simple misdirection from dramatic to mundane. Functional but predictable.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Is this blood?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: It's just a ceiling leak.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: A ceiling leak? That's it?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm afraid so. Very mundane.

jokes.text: What are you wearing? What is that? A nightgown? / It's a nightshirt, David.
jokes.setup: David questions Johnny's sleepwear
jokes.punchline: Johnny defensively corrects the terminology
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing David's snark and Johnny's wounded pride over something trivial.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Dad, are you wearing pajamas?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: These are not pajamas. This is sleepwear.

jokes.text: You might want to rethink the nightgown first. There's an ebenezer scrooge thing happening right now.
jokes.setup: Johnny wants to confront hotel staff about the leak
jokes.punchline: David suggests he looks like Scrooge in his nightshirt
jokes.explanation: Sharp visual observation with perfect character voice. David's wit cutting through Johnny's anger.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm going to go down there and give them a piece of my mind about that leak.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, you might want to change first. You look like Scrooge McDuck in that nightshirt.

jokes.text: Oh my God, that's who I was thinking of. / My best to Bob Cratchit.
jokes.setup: Johnny realizes the Scrooge comparison is apt
jokes.punchline: David delivers the Cratchit reference as a parting shot
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback that extends the metaphor. David's literary wit shines.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, maybe the comparison is apt.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, and unlike Scrooge, you don't even have Tiny Tim to feel bad about.

jokes.text: This bell is broken. / Yeah, sorry. It's more or less decorative.
jokes.setup: Johnny tries to ring for service
jokes.punchline: Stevie's casual admission that the bell doesn't work
jokes.explanation: Stevie's deadpan delivery of the motel's dysfunction. Establishes character and setting.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Excuse me? Hello?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah, that bell doesn't work.

jokes.text: He's a good guy, he's just a big drinker.
jokes.setup: Stevie is calling a plumber
jokes.punchline: Casual mention that the plumber has a drinking problem
jokes.explanation: Shows the town's low standards through Stevie's matter-of-fact delivery.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Hi, yes, I need a plumber as soon as possible. My sink is backing up and it's really gross.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: No, I don't think that's going to work for me because you sound drunk.

jokes.text: He's the best one. He's also the only one so...
jokes.setup: Johnny asks about a real estate agent named Ray
jokes.punchline: Stevie reveals Ray is the 'best' only because he's the only option
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan irony that deflates Johnny's expectations about having choices.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Is Ray a good real estate agent?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: He's the best we have.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: That's not really an answer.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: He's the only one we have.

jokes.text: Yeah... unfortunately, due to a lack of everything, we don't do room service.
jokes.setup: Johnny requests elaborate room service
jokes.punchline: Stevie's blunt explanation of the motel's limitations
jokes.explanation: 'Due to a lack of everything' is a perfect encapsulation of the motel's poverty through Stevie's dry wit.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'd like to place a room service order.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: We don't have room service.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, what if I wanted something brought to my room?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: You'd have to go get it yourself. Or ask one of us, but we'd probably say no.

jokes.text: I'd rather drink the pipe-water. / I hear ya.
jokes.setup: Stevie mentions the complimentary espresso bar
jokes.punchline: Johnny prefers contaminated water and Stevie agrees
jokes.explanation: Johnny's disgust escalated and Stevie's casual agreement shows how bad things really are.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'd rather drink contaminated water.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah, that tracks.

jokes.text: He said he'd never date someone with a toe ring and yet...
jokes.setup: Alexis looking at her ex's social media
jokes.punchline: The hypocrisy of her ex's dating preferences
jokes.explanation: Shows Alexis's petty obsession with her ex. Character-specific pain.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, look at this. He's got all these requirements on his dating profile. 'Must be ambitious, must have their life together, must be financially independent.'

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: This is the same man who asked me to co-sign a lease for his apartment because his credit score was literally a cry for help.

jokes.text: Rub my back. / What?! No. / I rubbed your back many a nights when you were little. / Yeah, in exchange for half my allowance.
jokes.setup: Johnny asks David to rub his back like old times
jokes.punchline: David reveals Johnny charged him for back rubs as a child
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character revelation that Johnny was entrepreneurial even with parental affection. Perfect escalation and character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: David, could you rub my back like you used to?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Like I used to? Dad, you charged me five dollars a rub.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, you had very tense shoulders.

jokes.text: she has hakuna matata tattooed on her foot. / A Disney tattoo shouldn't be a surprise.
jokes.setup: Alexis is horrified by her ex's new girlfriend's tattoo
jokes.punchline: David's deadpan observation about the ex dating escorts
jokes.explanation: David's matter-of-fact acknowledgment of his sister's poor choice in men with perfect deadpan timing.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, did you see her tattoo? It's a whole back piece!

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, yes, I did see it. And I have to say, it's not the most offensive thing about her.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: What do you mean?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I mean, the fact that she's dating your ex is really the bigger issue here.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: David!

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What? I'm just saying, based on some of the Instagram photos I've seen, I'm fairly certain he's been dating several other women as well. Which, you know, either makes him a polygamist or he's been patronizing a very successful escort service. Either way, the tattoo is the least of our concerns.

jokes.text: Actually, now that I think about it, where are those friends? / They're just giving me space right now. / Because they're considered people.
jokes.setup: David questions where Alexis's friends are
jokes.punchline: David's cutting remark about her friends being accessories
jokes.explanation: Devastating sibling burn that reveals the shallow nature of Alexis's social circle. Economy and precision are perfect.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Where are all your friends?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: They're busy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Busy being accessories that you've discarded.

jokes.text: We actually both decided that it was best that he see other people so...
jokes.setup: David says it's best that her ex dumped her
jokes.punchline: Alexis's delusional spin on being dumped
jokes.explanation: Classic denial/face-saving behavior that's very true to Alexis's character, though predictable.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: He didn't dump me. He just realized that he couldn't handle dating someone who was out of his league. It was actually very mature of him.

jokes.text: You are blind to reality and for that I am most proud
jokes.setup: Moira interrupting the siblings' bickering
jokes.punchline: Moira celebrates delusion as a virtue
jokes.explanation: Perfect Moira line - theatrical, absurd logic delivered with maternal pride. Uniquely hers.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I don't know what you all are arguing about, but I want you to know that I have always found delusion to be the most wonderful of all virtues. It has gotten me through some very dark times.

jokes.text: but our worlds evil twin... has reared her ugly... / Okay. I'm taking my journal into the bathroom. Where I will be shutting the door.
jokes.setup: Moira begins an elaborate metaphor
jokes.punchline: David cuts off her speech and escapes to bathroom
jokes.explanation: David's practical escape from Moira's theatrical tendencies. Good family dynamic humor.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Life is like a beautiful tapestry, woven with threads of—

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm going to the bathroom.

jokes.text: Oh, I love your complexion.
jokes.setup: Meeting Ray for the first time
jokes.punchline: Moira's inappropriate personal comment as greeting
jokes.explanation: Shows Moira's lack of social boundaries and theatrical personality through awkward compliment.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Hello, you must be Ray. I have to say, for someone in the real estate business, you have remarkably small hands.

jokes.text: Mr. Rose and I would like to put a for sale sign on this incredible town.
jokes.setup: Ray asks what he can help with
jokes.punchline: Moira calling the terrible town 'incredible' while wanting to sell it
jokes.explanation: Moira's sarcastic description of their terrible situation with theatrical flair.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, Ray, I'm so glad you asked. We'd like to put the house on the market.

dialogue.speaker: Ray
dialogue.line: Oh! Well, that's wonderful news!

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, well, we've decided that this town is absolutely incredible, and we'd like to share that incredible discovery with other people by selling our home and leaving immediately.

jokes.text: You know, because of the humiliating... / Yes, we know. We know. We know.
jokes.setup: Ray starts to explain why the government repossessed their assets
jokes.punchline: Johnny's desperate attempt to stop the painful reminder
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment as Ray inadvertently twists the knife in their public humiliation.

dialogue.speaker: Ray
dialogue.line: Well, the government seized your assets because you failed to pay your taxes for—

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Ray, Ray, Ray! Do you have any gum?

jokes.text: I need to confer with the financial powerhouse of the family.
jokes.setup: Johnny needs to discuss business with Moira
jokes.punchline: Calling the theatrical Moira the 'financial powerhouse'
jokes.explanation: Johnny's sarcastic reference to Moira's role in their financial decisions, given their current situation.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Moira, we need to discuss the finances. You're the financial powerhouse around here.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, of course I am, darling. I've always had a head for numbers. Very left-brain of me.

jokes.text: Son of a bitch!
jokes.setup: Ray shows them fine print about needing mayor's approval
jokes.punchline: Johnny's explosive realization they're trapped
jokes.explanation: Basic reaction humor. Johnny's frustration is understandable but the moment isn't particularly crafted.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. We need the mayor's approval? The mayor of this town?

dialogue.speaker: Ray
dialogue.line: Yes, that's what the fine print says.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Ray, the mayor of this town tried to sell me a timeshare at a gas station. We are trapped here. We are trapped in this town, forever.

jokes.text: No, that's just the way they smell.
jokes.setup: David complains his sheets smell like cigarettes
jokes.punchline: Stevie's matter-of-fact acceptance of the terrible condition
jokes.explanation: Perfect Stevie deadpan delivery accepting the motel's substandard conditions as normal.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Stevie, these sheets smell like cigarettes.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, welcome to the Rosebud.

jokes.text: I just pushed the bed against the wall and put down a bucket where the bed was.
jokes.setup: David asks about the plumber
jokes.punchline: Stevie's absurdly inadequate 'solution' to the leak
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly absurd non-solution delivered with Stevie's characteristic matter-of-fact tone.

jokes.text: I don't even know what 'tailgate' means. In my mind I'm picturing like a Klan rally.
jokes.setup: Stevie invites David to a tailgate party
jokes.punchline: David's dramatic misunderstanding escalates to worst-case scenario
jokes.explanation: Perfect David paranoia and city-dweller's fear of rural culture, escalated to absurd extreme.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Hey, do you want to go to a tailgate party with me?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: A tailgate party? Stevie, I don't even own a vehicle with a tail gate.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: It's just a party at the parking lot before the game.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Before the game? So we're just standing in a parking lot? In the cold? With strangers?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah, basically.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: And what if someone hits a ball and it comes through the windshield? What if there's a pile-up? What if I get separated from you and can't find my way back to civilization? Stevie, I could die in a parking lot.

jokes.text: Yeah, just fewer pointy hats.
jokes.setup: David's Klan rally comparison
jokes.punchline: Stevie plays along with the comparison casually
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan callback that doesn't correct David's paranoia but playfully confirms his fears.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: This is worse than a Klan rally.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah, I went to one of those once. This is definitely worse.

jokes.text: I'm not really in the mood to be a victim of a hate crime tonight so...
jokes.setup: Declining the party invitation
jokes.punchline: David's dramatic escalation of small-town party to hate crime
jokes.explanation: David's anxiety about rural culture reaching peak paranoid comedy. Very character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I appreciate the invitation, but I'm going to have to decline.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: A small-town party in a backyard with a keg and people I don't know? That's not a party, that's a hate crime.

jokes.text: Um, yes. Love that journey for me.
jokes.setup: Stevie mentions the party to both siblings
jokes.punchline: Alexis's self-absorbed response in trendy language
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Alexis's self-absorption through millennial speak.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, a party? That's literally so fetch. I mean, I would totally go, but I just got my nails done and I'm not trying to chip them before my Instagram photoshoot. Plus, everyone there would probably just want to talk about me anyway, so like, I'm basically doing them a favor by not showing up.

jokes.text: Maybe a neck tattoo would be cute. / No, it wouldn't.
jokes.setup: Alexis describes her ideal rebound guy
jokes.punchline: David's immediate, flat rejection of her taste
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective sibling dynamic - David's immediate shutdown of Alexis's questionable preferences.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: He'd be like, really tan, and have great hair, and just like, not care about anything.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: So you're describing a golden retriever.

jokes.text: So Johnny Rose wants the ol' Johnny Hancock, huh?
jokes.setup: Roland discussing the contract signature
jokes.punchline: Roland's terrible pun on Johnny's name and 'John Hancock'
jokes.explanation: Weak pun that establishes Roland's personality as someone who thinks he's funnier than he is.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You just need to sign here, Johnny. Put your John Hancock right there.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: It's just Johnny Rose.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, that's what I said — Johnny Hancock!

jokes.text: You're not rejecting my hospitality, are you, Johnny? Because that really wouldn't be a good way to start things off, I don't think.
jokes.setup: Johnny tries to keep it business-only
jokes.punchline: Roland's thinly veiled threat disguised as hospitality
jokes.explanation: Great power play from Roland, showing small-town politics through passive-aggressive hospitality.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You know, Johnny, I'd hate to see anything happen to your business. These things are so fragile, you know? One bad word from the right person, and boom, it's gone. But hey, that's not gonna happen to you, right? Because we're friends.

jokes.text: Johnny, you want that, to get that you need this.
jokes.setup: Johnny still trying to avoid dinner
jokes.punchline: Roland's crude quid pro quo delivered like a gangster
jokes.explanation: Roland establishing his power over the Roses through forced social obligation.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Look, I scratch your back, you scratch mine. You come to dinner, I make sure the permits for your store go through without a hitch. Capisce?

jokes.text: obviously she'd be super devastated if I bailed last minute
jokes.setup: Alexis explaining why she can't come to dinner
jokes.punchline: Acting like Stevie would be heartbroken about a casual invitation
jokes.explanation: Alexis's self-importance making her think everyone desperately needs her presence.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I can't come to dinner tonight because I have an audition tomorrow and I need to prepare.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I mean, Stevie would be absolutely devastated if I just casually showed up without giving her weeks of notice. She'd probably cry for days.

jokes.text: Davis, you've got nothing on tonight. / How... how do you know that. / What do you have on tonight? / What?
jokes.setup: Johnny assuming David has no plans
jokes.punchline: David's inability to come up with any social plans
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment as David's lack of social life is exposed in real time.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: So what are your plans for tonight?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I don't have any plans.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, why don't you go out? See some friends?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I don't really have friends to see.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, what about a date? A movie? Anything?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm just going to stay in and organize my closet.

jokes.text: It's a dance. And sometimes you lead. and sometimes you follow. / And which are you doing now, John?
jokes.setup: Johnny explains business as a dance metaphor
jokes.punchline: Moira cutting through his BS with direct question
jokes.explanation: Moira puncturing Johnny's attempt to save face with elegant directness.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Business is like a dance, Moira. You have to know when to lead, when to follow, when to pivot...

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Do you have any money?

jokes.text: Following in the game but leading in the dance, right? And he thinks he's leading in the dance, but only because I've allowed him to think...
jokes.setup: Johnny tries to explain his business strategy
jokes.punchline: Johnny getting completely tangled in his own metaphor
jokes.explanation: Johnny digging himself deeper into confused metaphor shows his need to maintain dignity despite powerlessness.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I like to think of business as a ship, you see. You're the captain, navigating the treacherous waters of commerce. You need a strong crew, a solid hull, and you always keep one hand on the wheel.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: But then the crew mutinies, the hull springs a leak, and suddenly you're swimming with the sharks instead of sailing over them.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Which is why I've decided to abandon ship entirely and take up hot air ballooning as a business model instead.

jokes.text: And if I bang my head and slip beneath the surface, so be it, Mr. Rose.
jokes.setup: Moira saying she'll go bathe
jokes.punchline: Moira's theatrical contemplation of drowning in the bath
jokes.explanation: Peak Moira drama - turning a simple bath into theatrical suicide contemplation. Perfectly character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm going to go bathe. And if I happen to drown, it will have been a choice.

jokes.text: Apparently the plumber's in rehab.
jokes.setup: Update on the plumber situation
jokes.punchline: Casual revelation that their 'good guy' plumber is now in rehab
jokes.explanation: Good callback that escalates the plumber's problems from 'big drinker' to 'in rehab'.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: So, how did the plumbing situation turn out?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Oh, our guy's in rehab.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah, he seemed like such a nice guy too.

jokes.text: David, for the gate party tonight is it more of a leather pants thing or... / Son of a bitch!
jokes.setup: Alexis asking David about party attire
jokes.punchline: Johnny's explosive reaction interrupting the fashion consultation
jokes.explanation: Good timing of Johnny's continued frustration cutting through Alexis's trivial concerns.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: David, what do you wear to a party?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, it depends on the venue and the dress code, but typically something that's—

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: CLOTHES! You wear CLOTHES to a party!

jokes.text: You have a really lovely home. It's really um, understated. / Thank you. I get a lot of my ideas from magazines. / Don't be modest. This is 100 percent you and only you.
jokes.setup: Moira complimenting Jocelyn's clearly tacky home
jokes.punchline: Moira's backhanded compliments about the obvious magazine copying
jokes.explanation: Moira's theatrical politeness masking obvious disdain for the tacky decor. Good class comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Your home is absolutely charming. Is this all from a magazine?

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Yes! Do you like it?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I can certainly tell. The way you've arranged everything... it's very... intentional. Like someone followed instructions very carefully.

jokes.text: It's the treat that keeps our love life percolating. It's like an aphrodisiac with him.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn serving cheese ball
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn's overshare about cheese ball's effect on Roland's libido
jokes.explanation: Standard cringe humor from inappropriate oversharing at dinner party.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: I made this cheese ball with Roland in mind. He just loves it. It really gets him going, if you know what I mean. Last time I served it, we didn't even make it to dessert, if you catch my drift.

jokes.text: she would walk into the space wearing a clay mask of a fawn, remove her clothing and breast feed members of the audience. It was a commentary on income inequality.
jokes.setup: David describing his gallery's last show
jokes.punchline: Increasingly absurd performance art with pretentious justification
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation of pretentious art world absurdity. Perfect David character moment and hilarious concept.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: The last show we had was a performance art piece where a woman stood completely still for six hours while someone read the phone book to her.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: The artist's statement said it was a commentary on the commodification of human attention in late-stage capitalism.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Before that, we had a man who filled the entire gallery with bubble wrap and just walked around popping it. Very slowly. For three days.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: He called it 'a visceral exploration of anxiety and the fragility of existence.'

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: My personal favorite was the artist who submitted a single blank canvas with a certificate of authenticity that simply said 'invisible to the naked eye, visible to the enlightened mind.'

jokes.text: Wow... Heady stuff.
jokes.setup: David's description of bizarre performance art
jokes.punchline: Roland's understated response to the shocking description
jokes.explanation: Perfect understated reaction to David's outrageous art description. Great economy and timing.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: He suspended himself from the ceiling with fishing line and covered his entire body in vegetable oil and feathers, then he just... hung there. Making these guttural sounds for forty-five minutes.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Was it good?

jokes.text: I wish I could pull off the whole gloomy, no makeup look. It's so French.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn talking to Moira
jokes.punchline: Calling Moira's dramatic look 'French' and 'no makeup'
jokes.explanation: Jocelyn's complete misreading of Moira's heavily made-up theatrical appearance.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Oh, that's very French. Very chic. Like you're not wearing any makeup at all.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm wearing a full face.

jokes.text: A town this size, you've either been through 'em or know a little too much about 'em.
jokes.setup: Alexis asking about being single
jokes.punchline: Stevie's cynical assessment of small-town dating
jokes.explanation: Sharp observation about small-town dating limitations delivered with Stevie's characteristic cynicism.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Well, in a town this size, your options are basically: taken, gay, or your cousin. And honestly, the cousin thing is still on the table for some people here.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: So being single isn't really a choice, it's more of a default setting while you wait for someone acceptable to become available or move to town.

jokes.text: this swan is best when she flies solo.
jokes.setup: Alexis going to work the party
jokes.punchline: Alexis's pretentious self-description as a swan
jokes.explanation: Perfect Alexis self-aggrandizement through ridiculous animal metaphor.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm like a swan. You know, I glide effortlessly across the surface, but underneath, my legs are paddling like crazy.

jokes.text: I tried reading that thing it is really boring.
jokes.setup: Johnny asking about the contract
jokes.punchline: Roland admitting he didn't actually read the legal document
jokes.explanation: Shows Roland's casual approach to his mayoral duties and lack of professionalism.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland, did you actually read through the entire contract before you signed it?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Read it? Johnny, I looked at it. I saw the words. I didn't understand any of them, but I saw them.

jokes.text: I don't think it's gonna pass. I don't think it's passing. / Migraines can be so awful. / Yeah, well, not this one. Not this one.
jokes.setup: David claiming to have a migraine to escape dinner
jokes.punchline: David's increasingly desperate fake symptoms
jokes.explanation: David's transparent attempt to escape the dinner party through fake illness. Good cringe comedy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I have a migraine.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh David, that's terrible. We'll reschedule.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Actually, it's getting worse. I think I'm also experiencing some vertigo.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Should we call a doctor?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No, no. I'm also fairly certain I've lost feeling in my left foot. And my tongue tastes purple.

jokes.text: Why don't you lay down on our mattress. The sheets are in the dryer but there is rubber pad.
jokes.setup: David trying to leave with fake migraine
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn's offer to let him lie on bare rubber padding
jokes.explanation: Jocelyn's well-meaning but horrifying suggestion reveals their household's dysfunction.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I think I'm getting a migraine. I should probably head home.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Oh no! You can lie down in the back room. We have a cot with some really nice bare rubber padding.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's... that's very kind, but I think I'll just—

jokes.text: I brought vodka as a house gift. / I don't see it.
jokes.setup: Alexis mentioning her contribution to the party
jokes.punchline: Stevie's observation that the vodka is conspicuously absent
jokes.explanation: Implies Alexis already drank the vodka she brought as a gift. Stevie's dry observation.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I brought vodka.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah, where is it?

jokes.text: Well, my sister texted me 'help'. And my mind went straight to deliverance. So I wasn't too far off.
jokes.setup: David explaining why he came to the party
jokes.punchline: David's paranoid reference to the movie Deliverance
jokes.explanation: Perfect David paranoia escalated to horror movie reference. Very character-specific fear.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I came because Moira said there would be a lot of influential people here, and I thought it might be good for business. But now I'm realizing this is basically the setup to Deliverance, and I'm not interested in being banjos'd.

jokes.text: That's funny because your sister texted me from across the bonfire and said you'd probably show up here because you were bored and lonely and had nothing better to do.
jokes.setup: David's heroic narrative about saving Alexis
jokes.punchline: Stevie revealing Alexis's actual assessment of David's social life
jokes.explanation: Stevie puncturing David's heroic delusion with harsh reality from his sister.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah, Alexis told me that your social life makes her feel less lonely.

jokes.text: or you want to finish high school? / I'm not sure, I couldn't hear over your husband's chewing.
jokes.setup: Moira asking about Jocelyn's teaching
jokes.punchline: Moira's cutting insult disguised as mishearing
jokes.explanation: Devastating Moira insult disguised as confusion. Perfect economy and character assassination.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: How is your teaching going, Jocelyn?

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Oh, it's been wonderful, Moira. The children are really responding well to my methods.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: That's lovely. And tell me, how are you finding the work... exhausting?

jokes.text: Although I know those boys are undressing me every time I drop a piece of chalk.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn talking about teaching high school
jokes.punchline: Inappropriate comment about student fantasies
jokes.explanation: Standard cringe humor from inappropriate teacher behavior, but fairly predictable.

jokes.text: I didn't want to say anything, but you were my favourite character on 'Sunrise Bay'.
jokes.setup: Roland recognizing Moira's 'bitch face'
jokes.punchline: Roland admitting he was a fan of her soap opera
jokes.explanation: Shows Roland's low-brow entertainment preferences and Moira's former fame.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I know that look. That's your 'bitch face.' I recognize it from when you played Dr. Clara Mandrake on 'The Crows Have Eyes III.'

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: It was a limited series.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, I was a fan.

jokes.text: I could be doing cartwheels in a thong in front of that television, which I have done, but if your show was on, I was as good as wall paper.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn talking about Roland's devotion to the show
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn's overshare about doing cartwheels in a thong
jokes.explanation: Typical cringe overshare, memorable for its absurdity but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Roland is so devoted to that show. He watches it every single night, and he gets so invested in the characters.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Last week, one of the characters had a baby, and Roland cried for like twenty minutes. It was actually really sweet.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: I got so excited about it that I did cartwheels around the living room in just a thong. Roland didn't even notice because he was so focused on the show!

jokes.text: I will go ahead and sign off on the sale... For a slap.
jokes.setup: Roland agreeing to sign the contract
jokes.punchline: Roland's bizarre request to be slapped by the soap opera actress
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly absurd power play that's both creepy and funny. Great escalation of Roland's weird behavior.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Yeah, I'll sign it. But I got one condition.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I want to be slapped by a soap opera actress.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: It's been a dream of mine.

jokes.text: We've role-played it like a thousand times, but it's not the real thing.
jokes.setup: Roland explaining his slap request
jokes.punchline: Admitting he and Jocelyn role-play Moira's characters
jokes.explanation: Escalates the creepiness factor of Roland's request with TMI about their role-playing.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, Jocelyn and I, we like to... we like to role-play. And sometimes we like to role-play as some of Moira's characters.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: So when I ask you to slap me, I'm not asking you to slap me. I'm asking you to slap... Vivienne Darkbloom.

jokes.text: Sign the fucking contract!
jokes.setup: The slapping debate continues
jokes.punchline: Moira finally snapping and swearing at Roland
jokes.explanation: Perfect payoff to Moira's mounting frustration. The contrast between her theatrical speech and blunt profanity is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh, for fuck's sake, Roland!

jokes.text: this has been a lot of fun, but I'm starting to smell a little too much like uh, campfire, and denim and plaid and stuff so... bye.
jokes.setup: Alexis leaving the tailgate party
jokes.punchline: Her snobbish listing of 'unacceptable' smells from normal people
jokes.explanation: Perfect Alexis snobbery about normal rural life smells. Very character-specific entitlement.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I cannot be here. The smell of... is that beer? And barbecue? And... is someone wearing cologne from a gas station? This is completely unacceptable.

jokes.text: Your choices are beer or beer.
jokes.setup: David asking about drink options
jokes.punchline: Stevie's blunt assessment of the limited beverage menu
jokes.explanation: Classic Stevie deadpan about the party's limitations. Simple but effective.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What drink options do you have?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: We have water, and... well, that's it. That's the options.

jokes.text: I assume a pint glass is out of the question?
jokes.setup: David accepting beer as his only option
jokes.punchline: David's hopeful inquiry about proper glassware at a tailgate
jokes.explanation: David's continued inability to adjust his expectations to rural reality.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Do you have a pilsner glass?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: A snifter? Anything with a stem?

jokes.text: Just open your throat. And go down, 'cause you're tall. / Just widen your throat!
jokes.setup: Stevie teaching David to use a beer bong
jokes.punchline: Stevie's increasingly desperate instructions about throat technique
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy of David's incompetence at basic party skills. Mildly funny but predictable.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Okay, so you just relax your throat and let it flow down.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I am relaxing my throat.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: No, you're not. You're doing that thing where you're tensing up. Just... open your throat more.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I don't know how to open my throat more, Stevie.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: It's like you're swallowing, but you're not swallowing. You're just... letting it happen.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That makes no sense.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Okay, imagine you're a bird. You're a bird and the beer is just going down into your bird body.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Why would I imagine that?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Just do it! Be the bird, David!

jokes.text: This is my town and you're gonna play by my rules. Comprende? / I'm just messing with you, man! Come on! / Or am I? / No. I am.
jokes.setup: Roland finally signing the contract
jokes.punchline: Roland's power-play followed by immediate backtracking and confusion
jokes.explanation: Good Roland character moment showing his petty power and immediate uncertainty about his own authority.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I'm not signing this until you agree to my terms.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Actually, wait. What are the terms? What are we talking about here?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You know what, never mind. I'll just sign it. Is this the line? Do I sign here?

jokes.text: I mean, most people just thought it was a waste of money that it would never sell, you know? Who in their right mind would buy it! Then along came you.
jokes.setup: Roland casually mentioning the town was on market for 20 years
jokes.punchline: Roland's oblivious insult about the Roses being crazy to buy it
jokes.explanation: Devastating revelation delivered with Roland's characteristic obliviousness to his own insults.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, this town's been on the market for twenty years. You'd have to be crazy to buy it.

jokes.text: This is the dark side of the moon, Johnny. And we'll be here another 20 years, tucked in a crater, lost to all.
jokes.setup: Learning the town was unsellable for 20 years
jokes.punchline: Moira's theatrical despair using space metaphors
jokes.explanation: Perfect Moira catastrophizing with beautiful, over-the-top metaphor. Peak theatrical despair.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Twenty years? That's not a real estate problem, that's a cosmic phenomenon. We didn't just move to a town, we've entered a black hole of property values. I'm not upset, I'm scientifically devastated.

jokes.text: You're being super loud right now. I can hear you thought the headphones! So if you can just wrap it up in here. 10 to a 5. Thanks.
jokes.setup: Johnny's dramatic family rally speech
jokes.punchline: Stevie's practical interruption treating it like a noise complaint
jokes.explanation: Perfect puncture of Johnny's dramatic moment with Stevie's practical, service-industry authority. Great deflation.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We are a family! And families stick together through thick and thin! We will not be broken by circumstance or hardship! We will rise above this adversity and show this town what the Rose family is made of!

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: You know, some of us are trying to sleep.

jokes.text: Good riddance, is what they said.
jokes.setup: David describing his job at the grocery store
jokes.punchline: David revealing he was fired and the grocery store's reaction
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing David's unsuitability for normal employment with his wounded pride.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I was fired from the grocery store.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: They said I was 'too judgmental' with the customers.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Which is ironic, because I was nothing but professional.

tmdb_overview: Johnny must get permission to sell the town from its Mayor, Roland Schitt. David and Alexis spend a night out with the locals.
insight: Fish-out-of-water setup lands 67 jokes in 24 minutes, establishing the family's clueless desperation.
